extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. The property requires globally(opted_out implies not sent), i.e., no reachable state has opted_out=true and sent=true.

The transition [t_resume_glitch] on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 10): sent := true directly violates this: it sets sent:=true while opted_out is true. Its guard (opted_out and ticks>=10) is reachable.

Witness execution (12 events, within the 14-event bound):
- 10x tick: ticks saturates at 10 (ticks := if ticks<10 then ticks+1 else ticks). Each tick also sets sent:=false. State: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=10.
- optout: t_optout fires (guard true), opted_out:=true, sent:=false. State: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=10.
- send_attempt: guard of t_resume_glitch (opted_out=true and ticks=10>=10) holds, so sent:=true. State: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=10.

The final state has opted_out=true AND sent=true, falsifying (opted_out implies not sent), so globally(...) fails. Even though t_send_blocked (guard opted_out) is also enabled on this send_attempt, under standard nondeterministic transition semantics any enabled transition may fire, so the glitch transition is a valid firing and produces the violation. The ticks must be advanced before the final send_attempt because the tick update resets sent:=false; ordering ticks then optout then send_attempt avoids any subsequent reset.
